coredata.dc:title: Nanoporous graphene quantum dots constructed from nanoribbon superlattices with controllable pore morphology and size for wastewater treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Abdelsalam, Hazem
Sakr, Mahmoud A.S.
Saroka, Vasil A.
Abd-Elkader, Omar H.
Zhang, Qinfang
coredata.dc:description: The pollution of water bodies with toxic components possesses a dangerous impact on human health and the environment. Therefore, it is vital to find efficient materials for water treatment. Herein we construct various nanoporous graphene quantum dots from nanoribbon superlattices with tunable pore size and abundant active sites. The investigations are performed using density functional theory calculations. Depending on the type of superlattice, the pore size can be tuned from 3 Å to 11 Å and the pore morphologies are also controllable. Some of the nanoporous graphenes have edge states at the zigzag terminations that decrease the band gap and act as active sites. While others do not have edge states due to the armchair terminations and form semiconductors with an energy gap of ∼ 2.7 eV Molecular electrostatic potential confirms that edge carbon atoms have high negative electronic densities and thus they are suitable for intermolecular coordination with the toxic metals. The considered 2D membranes show good capability to adsorb heavy metals, namely Cd, Pb, and Hg, with negligible deformation. Additionally, attaching chemical groups or elements, namely CN, COOH, CS2, N, O, and S, to the pores not only increases the adsorption energy but also helps control the pore size. Therefore, with the enhanced adsorption energy and tunable pore size, the constructed nanoporous graphene quantum dots are ideal candidates for 2D membranes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoporous graphene quantum dots
Nanoribbons superlattices
DFT
Electronic properties
Heavy metals adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Nanoporous graphene quantum dots: construction and stability

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361 ), the Ministry of Science and Technology, Foreign Experts Program (No. QN2021014007L , QN2022014009L ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20211361 , 20KJA430004 ). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number ( RSP2023R468 ), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. VAS acknowledges support by EU HORIZON-MSCA-2021-PF-01 (project no. 101065500 , TeraExc).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optimized structures of nanoribbon superlattices: (a) A120-2-2 and (b) A60-4-3. (c-g) The finite 2D membranes with different pore sizes and edges.
Electronic density of states of different nanoporous graphenes, the insets show the corresponding HOMO distributions.
Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) maps for A120-PG, Z120-PG, Z120-N-PG, A60-PG, and R-PG.
The optimized structures of PG complexes with heavy metals (Top and side views).
Electronic density of states of selected nanoporous graphenes that are chemically modified at the pore edges.
The optimized structures (Front and side views) of functionalized PGs after adsorption of Cd (a–c), Hg (d–f), and Pb (g–h).
Natural charge, population, and electronic configuration for PG complexes.
Binding energy and energy gap of the chemically modified nanoporous graphene quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Nanoporous graphene quantum dots constructed from nanoribbon superlattices with controllable pore morphology and size for wastewater treatment

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pollution of water bodies with toxic components possesses a dangerous impact on human health and the environment. Therefore, it is vital to find efficient materials for water treatment. Herein we construct various nanoporous graphene quantum dots from nanoribbon superlattices with tunable pore size and abundant active sites. The investigations are performed using density functional theory calculations. Depending on the type of superlattice, the pore size can be tuned from 3 Å to 11 Å and the pore morphologies are also controllable. Some of the nanoporous graphenes have edge states at the zigzag terminations that decrease the band gap and act as active sites. While others do not have edge states due to the armchair terminations and form semiconductors with an energy gap of ∼ 2.7 eV Molecular electrostatic potential confirms that edge carbon atoms have high negative electronic densities and thus they are suitable for intermolecular coordination with the toxic metals. The considered 2D membranes show good capability to adsorb heavy metals, namely Cd, Pb, and Hg, with negligible deformation. Additionally, attaching chemical groups or elements, namely CN, COOH, CS2, N, O, and S, to the pores not only increases the adsorption energy but also helps control the pore size. Therefore, with the enhanced adsorption energy and tunable pore size, the constructed nanoporous graphene quantum dots are ideal candidates for 2D membranes.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions are dangerous pollutants. Independently of the type of their source of origin, natural or industrial, they end up in the water and air environments and pose a long-standing problem for human welfare [1–3]. This group of metals includes among others lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), zinc (Zn), mercury (Hg), arsenic (As), and even silver (Ag) and iron (Fe). Many heavy metal pollutants (As, Cd, Pb, and methylated forms of Hg) are poisonous even at low concentrations. Such metals play no role in human biochemistry and simply tend to bioaccumulation resulting in acute or chronic health problems. Environmental friendly, cost-efficient, and scalable methods of removing and monitoring heavy metal ions in ecosystems are of great interest for creating a safe environment worldwide [4].

Along with conventional materials such as activated carbons [5], clay minerals [6], polymers [7], zeolites [8], and mesoporous materials [9], graphene-based nanomaterials have been proposed for effective heavy metal pollution treatment [10,11]. Large surface area, variety of compatible functional groups, and strong adsorption capacity are the main advantages of this group of materials. Among the range of treatment techniques for the elimination of heavy metal ions from wastewater, membrane separation, and sorption technology are standing out. The former demonstrates high separation efficiency, but a high cost of maintenance in large-scale applications. While the latter is easy to perform and relatively inexpensive but requires improvement in efficiency. Graphene oxide [10] and hexagonal boron nitride [12] quantum dots have been proposed as good adsorbents of heavy metals when functionalized with proper functional groups [13]. Although the parameters of nanocomposites with quantum dots such as selective functionalization, dot size, etc. are gradually improving, a strategic menace is a problem with the separation of nanocomposites from aqueous solutions that affects recyclability, reversibility, and reusability of these materials.

Similar to graphene sponges, aerogels, and foams [11], graphene nanomeshes can overcome the separation difficulties of traditional graphene-based adsorbents from an aqueous solution after the adsorption process. In contrast, to nanometer-size quantum dots, graphene nanomeshes are macroscopically large, integral, and mechanically strong structures. As such they do not suffer from flocculation and aggregation. At the same time, they possess nanometer size pores and large edge areas. Such structures have been already proposed for similar sieving applications such as water desalinization and gas separation [13–15]. The ionic and molecular nanofiltration with graphene nanomeshes is possible due to their ability to transport water molecules while blocking solute ions or molecules; the high water permeance of such ultrathin membranes with minimum transport resistance and maximum performance makes them attractive for practical applications. Water permeance and the pore performance of the graphene nanomeshes are adjustable by the effective size and chemistry of the nanopores [16]. However, graphene nanomeshes are usually produced with O2 plasma etching, where pore size and structure control are only coarsely feasible.

Graphene nanoribbons are nanometer-width stripes of graphene [17–22]. They possess a range of attractive electronic [23–31], magnetic [24,32,33], optical [34–46], photovoltaic [47], and structural [48,49] properties. They exhibit astonishing structural variety of their edges that have been demonstrated to be achieved with atomic precision using bottom-up self-assembling of molecular precursors [50–52]. Over the past decade, a class of zigzag-shaped superlattices has expanded significantly. While the general classification of such structures is still a subject of future convention, systematic classifications based on the ribbon edge morphology and superlattice parameters have been proposed [26,27,30]. The species of four major classes of zigzag-shaped superlattices have been synthesized: A60 [53–55], A120 [56–58], Z60 [59], and Z120 [60], where A and Z stand for the ‘armchair’ and ‘zigzag’ edge respectively, while 60 and 120 denote the apex angle that is characteristic for the zigzag shape of the structure [26,27,30]. Moreover, Panighel et al. managed to synthesize stable edge-fluorinated graphene nanoribbons by a rational design of the building precursor which prevents the cleavage of the CF bond at the edges [61]. The fabrication of graphene nanoribbon heterojunctions through the combination of pristine and nitrogen-substituted hydrocarbon precursors has also been reported by Cai et al. [62]. The next level of self-organization has been demonstrated, whereby self-assembled ribbons laterally fuse [57,58,63,64]. When fusing ribbons have straight edges or compatible symmetry along their axis of translation they tend to increase their width [58,63]. However, zigzag-like structures can form atomically perfect and ideally ordered pores [57,58]. Recently, Moreno et al. reported a bottom-up method to synthesize nanoporous graphene (NPG) with controllable interribbon electronic coupling by the molecular bridges that couple the conducting nanoribbons [65,66]. This NPG not only has tunable pore size and interactive pore states but also a highly anisotropic electronic structure with an energy gap of ∼1 eV, rendering NPG a promising semiconductor for both sieving and electrical sensing applications. Additionally, the On-surface synthesis has been upraised to a totally different level and the atomically precise nanoporous heterogeneous junctions have been demonstrated [67]. Therefore, these emergent atomically sharp nanoporous graphene based on nanoribbons are potentially competitive with macroscopically ordered azatriangulene-based [68] or boroxine-linked [69] covalent organic frameworks.

Our recent systematic study of the covalent organic frameworks (COF) has shown a promising potential for Cd, Hg, and Pb adsorption after functionalization with CN, COOH, and NO2 [70]. Taking into account these results and recent advances in nanoporous graphene synthesis, we investigate the potential of currently available and prospective porous structures for the filtration of water and air pollution detectors with respect to harmful heavy metal ions.

In this study, a nanoporous graphene quantum dot is modeled as a finite-size system containing a sole pore of a regular shape. Here we focus on the in-pore chemistry and quantum properties of the pores, neglecting the macroscopic effects. This model should be valid for small and moderate concentrations of heavy metals when the active pores interacting with heavy metals can be considered to be independent and distributed non-periodically due to the stochastic nature of the interaction with atoms of metals. The calculations considered in this work were performed using density functional theory as implemented in Gaussian 16 [71]. The effective core potential “LANL2DZ” basis set was used to model the PG systems before and after the adsorption of metals because it shows an accurate description of the molecular properties of C-based materials before and after metal adsorption [72–74]. The functional used herein is the Becke-Three-Parameters-Lee-Yang-Parr hybrid functional (B3LYP) [75] since it shows a good representation of the electronic properties of C-based nanostructures [76,77]. The partial density of states was calculated using GaussSum software [78] based on the Gaussian output files.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanoporous graphene quantum dots: construction and stability
ce:section.ce:para: We consider graphene nanoribbon superlattices with different widths, lengths, and edge types to construct nanoporous graphene quantum dots with tunable pore size and pore edges as presented in Fig. 1 . The acronyms used in Fig. 1 are taken from our previous work on nanoribbon superlattices in Refs. [26,27,31,49]. Namely, A120-2-2 shown in Fig. 1(a) means an armchair superlattice, where the apex angle equals 120°, width equals 2 benzene rings and length equals 2 unit cells [49]. A60-4-3in Fig. 1(b) represents an armchair superlattice having an apex angle of 60° with a width equal to 4 dimer chains and a length equal to 3 unit cells [31]. Similarly, the Z120 and the narrow width Z120 (Z120-N) that are used to construct the nanoporous graphenes in Fig. 1 (e, f) can be found in Refs. [26,27]. We are going also to investigate the recently synthesized nanoporous graphene with the rubicene-type linkage of chevron graphene nanoribbons [64]. For porous structures, we have chosen zigzag-shaped superlattices that have been demonstrated experimentally with large enough superlattice periods, i.e. A60 [53–55], A120 [56–58], and Z120 [60]. Using such a strategy, we can construct nanoporous graphenes with various pore sizes as demonstrated in Fig. 1 (c–g). For brevity, hereafter we refer to the constructed nanoporous graphenes using a superlattice label supplemented with a ‘PG’ tag. For example, the nanoporous graphene quantum dots constructed from A120-2-2 superlattice is denoted as A120-PG. A similar convention applies to other structures in question. When a pore edge is functionalized with a chemical group we include the chemical group into the label of the structure. If we use, for example, the COOH group to modify the edge of A120-PG as shown in Fig. 1(h), then the structure label is A120-PG-COOH. Fig. 1 (h–j) demonstrates that further control over the pore size is obtained by attaching chemical groups.

The structure parameters such as the optimal bond length, bond angle, and dihedral angle are determined from the optimized A120-PG, A60-PG, Z120-PG, Z120-N-PG, R-PG, A120-PG-COOH, A60-PG-CN and R-PG-S shown in Fig. 1 (c-j). The CH and CC bond lengths fall between 1.079 Å to 1.509 Å CCC bond angles also range from 113.72 to 125.41 °, and the CCCC dihedral angle is around 180.00 °. The bond angles indicate that the type of hybridization is sp2. The dihedral angle values indicate that the surface of PGs is flat. The calculation of binding energy (Eb) per unit atom was performed using the equation Eb = (NCEC + (NH Ng)EH +NgEg - Et)/Nt, where NC, NH, Ng, and Nt represent the number of carbon atoms, number of hydrogen atoms, number of attached groups or elements, and the total number of atoms in the system, respectively. The total energies of the carbon atoms (EC), hydrogen atoms (EH), the attached group or element (Eg), and the final structure (Et) were used in the calculation. The values of Eb are 7.4, 7.2, 7.4, 7.6, and 7.7, 7.3, 7.1, and 7.6 eV for A120-PG, A60-PG, Z120-PG, Z120-N-PG, and R-PG, A120-PG-COOH, A60-PG-CN, and R-PG-S respectively. The positive values of Eb ensure the stable formation of the considered PG systems before and after chemical functionalization. Notably, the Eb value for R-PG is the highest among the studied systems, suggesting that the R-PG is the most stable compared to the other structures. After functionalization, Eb are also positive with almost the same values which indicates that the PGs preserve their stability after chemical modification, see Table 2 for all the cases of chemical modifications. To check the dynamical stability of the considered systems, frequency calculations were performed from which the vibrational frequencies can be obtained from the infrared (IR) spectra. The IR spectra for all the selected PGs show that all the vibrational frequencies responsible for the IR transitions are positive; see Fig. S1 of Appendix A. This indicates that there are no saddle points in the potential energy surface and all the structures are dynamically stable.

The thermal stability of the considered nanoporous graphenes is tested by performing ab initio molecular dynamics simulations. The ab initio molecular dynamics were performed using the atom-centered density matrix propagation (ADMP) molecular dynamics model [79,80]. The time evolution of the ground state energy and the bond lengths of the CC bond and C-chemical group bond have been simulated at 500 K for 5 femtoseconds. We check the thermal stability at this high temperature because an efficient adsorbent should adsorb the heavy metals at room temperature and desorbs them at a higher temperature, thus the adsorbent should be thermally stable at the desorption temperature. The trajectory showing the time evolution is presented in Figs. S2 and S3 in Appendix B. It is observed that the changes in ground state total energy and the bond lengths of all the considered adsorbents are negligible which confirms the thermal stability at 500 K.

Fig. 2 shows the electronic density of states (DOS) of the considered five nanoporous graphenes (a-e) and the corresponding distributions of the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO). The DOS is calculated using Gaussian distribution,
1
α
2 π
exp
[
−
(
∈ −
∈ i
)
2
2
α
2
]
for each molecular orbital ( ∈ i ) with broadening α = 0.032 eV. The finite 2D membranes considered here have different values of energy gaps: starting from 0.11 eV in R-PG to 2.73 eV in A120-PG. This difference in energy gaps in structures having almost the same number of atoms, such as A120-PG and A60-PG, is mainly a result of the edge type whether it is zigzag or armchair. The zigzag edges in graphene finite structures such as triangular nanographene hold unpaired electrons that cannot form pi- or sigma- bonds with neighbor C-atoms. Therefore, they form lower energy molecular orbitals that decrease the energy gap. It is worth noting that the values of energy gaps will be affected by considering spin-polarized calculations. However, since the main concern of this work is studying the adsorption capability of the nanoporous graphenes which is only slightly affected by spin-polarized calculations, we consider only the nonmagnetic calculations.

These half-filled edge states have been observed in all the considered systems, except for the A120-PG, and they are the reason for decreasing the energy gaps. The HOMO distribution can be used to understand the origin of bonds in that orbital. It can be delocalized on two C-atoms reflecting the origin from a pi-bond while a localized distribution indicates the origin from a lone pair or an unpaired electron on the atom. Since there are no lone pairs in the sp2 hybridized graphene, then the localized distributions observed in all the considered nanoporous graphenes except for A120-PG confirm their origin from the unpaired edge electrons, see Fig. 2 insets. From the HOMO/LUMO distribution, it is observed that these edge states are localized on the zigzag edges of the nanoporous graphenes. These edge electrons are highly interactive; thus they are promising for the adsorption of heavy metals. On the other hand, nanoporous graphene quantum dots with armchair terminations have all their electrons paired and the molecular orbitals distributions are extending on two or more atoms (see Fig. 2(a) for A120-PG). Therefore, the proper control of the energy gap and the density of states around the Fermi level by edge type greatly help to construct efficient nanoporous graphene with suitable conductivity and abundant active sites. Another important method to tune the electronic properties of nanoporous systems is mechanical strain [81]. In our case, applying in-plane tensile strain will result in the distinct deformation of nanoporous graphenes, which might have a significant effect on their surface activity. This interesting research point could be the subject of future work on these nanoporous graphenes.

Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) research, which helps anticipate reaction sites, reveals the designed A120-PG, Z120-PG, Z120-N-PG, A60-PG, and R-PG electrophilic versus nucleophilic sites. The MEPs are shown in Fig. 3 . The color scheme of the MEP surface is as follows: red and yellow indicate an electron-rich, blue and light blue indicate an electron-deficient, and green indicates neutral regions. The presence of a negative region surrounding the carbon atoms indicates that electrophilic attacks are occurring there. While the hydrogen atoms are located in the positive regions, nucleophiles can target these sites. The neutral sections are the areas of the molecule where there is no charge distribution. The carbon atoms that have high electron density are those atoms on the zigzag edges which hold localized electrons. For example, the red regions in Z120-PG are significantly higher than in A120-PG, this is because of the higher number of edge-localized electrons in the former. These red regions with high electron density are observed also in A60-PG or in general on carbon atoms at the zigzag edges. Therefore, the MEP confirms the results obtained from the electronic DOS that the sites at the zigzag edges are promising as active sites for sensing and catalytic applications. Consequently, the carbon atoms situated on the outer and inner boundaries are highly suitable for intermolecular coordination with toxic heavy metals, such as mercury (Hg), lead (Pb), and cadmium (Cd), and remove them from contaminated water.

The A60-PG, A120-PG, Z120-PG, and Z120-N-PG were tested for their ability to complex with heavy metals such as Hg, Pb, and Cd. Based on the MEP maps, we consider adsorption on the carbon atoms located on both the outer and inner boundaries because they exhibited a higher potential for intermolecular coordination with toxic heavy metals compared to surface atoms. The results in Fig. 4 show that some of the complexes maintained a flat surface, while others were slightly deformed.

The metal-PG lengths for all PG-complexes were within the range of 2.433→4.896 Å The Pb-C length was the shortest among A60-, A120-, Z120-, Z120-N-, and R-PG, as Pb forms chemical bonds compared to the physical bonds by Hg and Cd. The calculated adsorption energy (Eads) with negative values indicates that PG successfully adsorbs all of the considered heavy metals. Eads is defined as the difference between the ground state energy of the product (PG-metal) and reactants (PG and metals separately) with values ranging from −0.236 to −1.987 eV The Pb-PG complexes had the highest negative Eads values. Due to Pb's small size, its adsorption to PG structures was higher than that of Hg and Cd, as confirmed by its more negative adsorption energy values compared to the other complexes. The role of edge localized states can be observed here also where the adsorption energy of heavy metals by A120-PG is lower than that of other PGs as shown in Fig. 4. So now it is proven that localized electrons at the zigzag edges are active sites that enhance the adsorption properties of nanoporous graphene.

It is observed that we consider the adsorption of only one heavy metal on one PG to efficiently describe the interaction between the metal and C-atoms at the edges or pores. However, it is important also to study the maximum number of metal atoms that one PG can adsorb. For this point, taking into account the large computational power required, we consider Z120-N-PG as an example and study the maximum number of Cd atoms that it can adsorb. The numbers of adsorbed Cd atoms are arbitrarily chosen to be 3, 13, 23, 30, and 39 on one side and 26 on both sides (13 on each side) as shown in Fig. S4 of Appendix C. It is observed that the maximum number that one side can successfully adsorb with moderate adsorption energy, slight deformation, and no metal-metal clustering is 30 Cd atoms, see Fig. S4 (d). Increasing the number to 39 atoms, the metal atoms become very close to each other, and therefore metal-metal clusters are formed which prevent their adsorption as shown in Fig. S4 (e). Now since one side can adsorb 30 Cd, then it is expected that the other side will adsorb the same number and the total number of adsorbed metal atoms becomes 60 atoms. Due to the required high computational power and time, we tested only the case of 26 atoms on both sides as seen in Fig. S4 (f) which shows the suitable adsorption energy and negligible deformation. The adsorption capacity in weight (wt%) is calculated to estimate the percentage of adsorbed heavy metal with respect to the molecular weight of PGs,
wt %
=
n
W
C d
(
n
W
C d
+
W
P G
)
×
100
[82]. Where n is the number of adsorbed Cd, WCd is the molecular weight of the Cd atom, and WPG is the molecular weight of the Z120-N-PG. For adsorped 26 Cd-atoms, the adsorption capacity is 71.2 wt% and for 60 atoms it becomes 85.1 wt% which makes nanoporous graphene quantum dots ideal sorbents. The adsorption energy is calculated using the same method used for one atom with the addition of dividing by the number of the adsorbed metals. Therefore, PGs are potential applicants for heavy metal removal due to their high adsorption capacity, acceptable adsorption energy, negligible deformation, and without metal-metal clustering.

The determination of atomic charges is crucial in understanding the electrical properties of molecular systems. In this study, calculations were performed using the B3LYP/LanL2DZ level of theory to determine the natural atomic charge, natural population, and natural electronic configuration of metals in the studied PG complexes. The results in Table 1 indicate that the computed charge on the metals is different than the zero formal charge due to interactions with The PG. The magnitude of charge transfer observed in Cd and Hg is considerably lower than that of Pb. Consequently, the affinity of Pb for PG is greater in comparison to Hg and Cd. Table 1 also provides the electronic configuration for Cd, Hg, and Pb in all PG-based compounds. It shows that Hg and Pb coordinate with carbon atoms of the PG group through the empty orbital 6p orbitals, while the Cd ion coordinates via the empty orbital 5p orbitals.

The effect of chemical functionalization, by attaching chemical groups to C-atoms inside the pores, is investigated because it is expected to have significant effects on the pore size and the adsorption capability of the constructed 2D membranes. Three nanopores are considered as examples for studying chemical functionalization, namely A120-PG, A60-PG, and R-PG. Since there are several ways to enhance the activity of a material by chemical functionalization such as oxidation (introducing oxygen-containing groups, e.g. COOH, O, OH, etc.), nitrogenation, and sulfuration [83], we consider functionalization with COOH groups and O-atoms as examples of oxidation, CN and N for nitrogenation, and CS2 and S for sulfuration. The impact of these functional groups on the binding energy of PGs is investigated by calculating the binding energy (Eb), similar to the case of nonfunctionalized PGs. The values of Eb obtained for each system are presented in Table 2 , their positive values indicate that the PG-functional group systems are stable. Notably, R-PG-functional groups have the highest Eb value among the studied systems, suggesting that their molecular structure is the most stable. Additionally, the positive vibrational frequencies of the IR spectra for chosen functionalized PG (Fig. S1 Appendix A) confirm the geometrical stability after chemical modification.

The attached groups to C-atoms inside the pores have three main functions. (a) Tuning the electronic properties, for instance, decreasing the energy gap and increasing the electron density around the Fermi level leads to a noticeable boost in the adsorption capability. (b) decreasing the pore size which in turn improves the sieving properties of the nanographene, for instance, the large pore size of 11 Å in A120-PG can be decreased to 7 Å by attaching two COOH groups as seen in Fig. 1 (c, h). (c) enhancing the sorption capacity toward different pollutants by creating additional active sites. Starting with the electronic properties, the effect of chemical functionalization on the energy gap (Table 2) and the density of states (Fig. 5 ) show important modulations. The wide energy gap of A120-PG, Eg=2.73 eV, can be decreased to half (1.42 eV) by attaching two N-atoms or significantly decreases to 0.18/0.25 eV by attaching O/CS2 as given in Table. 1. This decrease in the energy gap will increase the conductivity which in turn enhances detecting the signal generated after the adsorption of heavy metal. Additionally, the electronic density of states shows a considerable increase in the intensity around the Fermi level for A120-PG after attaching CN and CS2 groups (Fig. 5a, b). These additional electronic states act as active sites that can bind with the pollutants and increase the adsorption energy. The decrease in the energy gap is not observed in the other membranes due to the existence of the low-energy edge states which could be more interactive than the states from the attached chemical groups. However, the density of states increases by chemical functionalization, for instance, the read peaks by the attached sulfur atoms in Fig. 5(d).

The effect of attaching the six chemical groups and elements on the adsorption of Cd by A60-PG is considered. The same is considered for the adsorption of Hg and Pb by A120-PG and R-PG, respectively. The results are divided into two parts, the first is shown in Fig. 6 and the second is shown in Fig. S5 Appendix D. The optimized structures of functionalized PGs after adsorption and the corresponding adsorption energies are depicted in Fig. 6. It is observed that the adsorption energy can be enhanced by chemical modification. For instance, the adsorption energy of Cd before functionalization (Eads=0.24 eV) can be increased up to 0.67 eV by attaching nitrogen-containing groups, see Fig. 6(a). The adsorption of Hg slightly increases also by the attached groups at the inner edges of A120-PG. The already high adsorption energy of Pb by R-PG (Eads=1.99 eV) can be further increased to 3.37 eV by attaching CS2 groups.

In summary, we construct various 2D nanoporous graphene quantum dots from nanoribbon superlattices with tunable pore size and morphology. The electronic properties of these 2D membranes and their interactions with heavy metals, Cd, Pb, and Hg, are investigated using DFT calculations. It is observed that the pore size can be smoothly controlled by changing the superlattice nanoribbons, pores with sizes ranging from 3 Å to 11 Å are obtained. The pore morphology can be also controlled to have zigzag termination with interactive edge states suitable for complexation with pollutants or armchair edges (no edge states), or mixed edges. These edge states decrease the energy gap in some cases to 0.11 eV While in other cases where these states disappear and the nanoporous graphene quantum dot is a semiconductor with an energy gap up to 2.7 eV The molecular electrostatic potential confirms that the edge carbon atoms have higher negative electronic densities than surface ones, thus a higher probability of heavy metal adsorption. Nanoporous graphene successfully adsorbs different toxic metals with appropriate adsorption energy. Attaching chemical groups increases the active states and provides additional control over the pore size. The adsorption strength can be further increased by attaching chemical groups to pores atoms. For instance, the adsorption energy of Cd is duplicated by attaching nitrogen-containing groups.

Hazem Abdelsalam: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration. Mahmoud A.S. Sakr: Visualization, Methodology, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Vasil A. Saroka: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Omar H. Abd-Elkader: Formal analysis, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Qinfang Zhang: Supervision, Methodology, Software, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361), the Ministry of Science and Technology, Foreign Experts Program (No. QN2021014007L, QN2022014009L), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211361, 20KJA430004). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2023R468), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. VAS acknowledges support by EU HORIZON-MSCA-2021-PF-01 (project no. 101065500, TeraExc).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2023.103109.
